speaker: interviewer
text: --would be my brother, and his name, and myself underneath. And, you know. And then you go around like-- or clockwise.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you say, and this picture was taken on the-- and you remember when it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know when it was taken. It was taken in 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you say this was sent to-- then you could say-- because it's part of your story--

speaker: interviewer
text: --how this was sent to your father in Slovakia.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is my grandfather, my mother's father. His name was [NON-ENGLISH] Spira [NON-ENGLISH]. He lived in Tylicz, which was a small town next to the-- in Poland, next to the Slovakian border.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture must have been taken sometimes in 1915 or 1920.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father. His name was Absz Nagel. This picture was taken during the war, around 1940, for one of the identification papers. Everybody had to have papers all the time, so-- and this is one-- one picture that we have left.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken, still in Krosno.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you-- how did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: We just found it after the war. I don't remember where. But possibly my brother found it some place.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Krosno during the war, probably 1940, to be sent to my father and older brother, Jack, to Slovakia. In the middle is my mother, Esther. Clockwise to the right is my brother Nathan, who didn't survive. That's me, the little girl. That's-- that's me. And that's my younger brother, Nathan, who was a year and a 1/2 younger. And to my left is my cousin, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: No, actually, you said both brothers named Nathan. One of them was Mordechai.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no, no, I'm sorry. Did I say both of them? No. The little one is Nathan, and the older one is Mordechai.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh no, I'm sorry. I'm all confused. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mordechai and-- no. One is Mordechai, and one is Nathan. Which is which?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the-- Nathan is the little one, and the older one is Nathan.

speaker: interviewer
text: You keep saying, this one was--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This picture was taken in Sweden, in the school where I stayed. Then I think it was called [NON-ENGLISH]. And these are my friends who were at the school there. There was nobody older than 18 there. They were-- all the girls were between 16 and 18. And I am to the left-- the girl on top to the left-- in the grey sweater, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year would this have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture was in 1946-- end of 1946, after I came out from the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here is my family before they were married. This is my husband, Victor. That's my middle son, Jeffrey, standing next to him. To the left, there's Robert. And the little one is Michael.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken before they were married. [LAUGHTER] I really don't know what year. I would say maybe 10 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right. And in-- was it taken in Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our oldest son, Robert Ungar, Dr. Robert Ungar. He's married to lovely Chani Taylor from New York. And this picture was taken in New York, I believe, 1983.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our middle son, Dr. Jeffrey Ungar. He's a physicist. And he was-- he was married in 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: '86. And he's married to a lovely girl from the Valley, from San Fernando Valley. Her name is Risa Silverstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken in Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our youngest son, Michael. He's a CPA and a-- a computer auditor. He lives now in Teaneck, New Jersey. He's married to this lovely Yaffa Kahn. And they have three lovely children. And they were married, I believe, in 1985.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my lovely four grandchildren. They belong to Robert and Chani. This picture was taken in 1994. I'll start in the middle, because she's my oldest granddaughter. Her name is Chaya. To the right is Dina. And this is Davi. And the little one is Shlomo.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. These three lovely ladies are my granddaughters. They belong to Michael and Yaffa. They live in Teaneck, New Jersey. From the right is Shoshana, the oldest. And then it's Elana. And the little one is [NON-ENGLISH]. This picture must have been taken two years ago, because she's four years old now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. These little angels-- they belong to my middle son, Jeffrey and Risa Ungar. The two older ones are twins. This is Esther and [NON-ENGLISH]. To my left is Ellie. And the little one is [NON-ENGLISH]. And they still have a younger sister. Her name is [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this picture must have been taken a year ago. That was in 1994.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is [NON-ENGLISH]. In this picture, she must be three-month-old, but she's a big girl now. She's one year old. And she is Jeffrey and Risa's youngest daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was 1994. This picture was--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I would like to introduce my family. This is my husband, Victor. And to the right is our oldest son, Robert, and his loving wife, Chani. And to my right is my oldest granddaughter, Chaya, Davi, Dina. And this is Shlomo.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's very nice to meet all of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we have to excuse their attire.

speaker: interviewee
text: They just walked in from school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Robert, tell me about what it was like growing up with-- as the children-- as a child of survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I grew up in the most wonderful environment that anyone could ever ask for. I have two of the most wonderful, warm, loving, caring parents. I think as a child of survivors, there was no-- no identity crisis in our house, because I knew who I was. I knew who I came form. My name is [NON-ENGLISH]. I'm named after my mother's father. And very often, even as a child, when I would think about what I would be doing, or should be doing, that lay very heavily on me that-- that really, I was, in a sense, my grandfather. And that, I think, molded me into what I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Ungar, you too are a survivor.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am. Yeah, I'm also a survivor.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm from Kosice. It's a-- it's a town in Eastern Slovakia. And I was not taken to a concentration camp. I was-- I was-- I lived on false papers as a non-Jew. And that's how I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were going to-- to get married, did you-- did you want to meet somebody and marry somebody who had had a similar experience to yours-- who was a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was important to me. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- how is-- how has being married to a woman who's as loving and outgoing as Gloria made your life different?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were happy, I guess. SHLOMO (GRANDSON): I want-- I want to go there. We never had any conflicts, and-- and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your message for the people who watch this tape? You survived as a little girl all by yourself. SHLOMO (GRANDSON): I want over there! What message do you have-- what have you learned-- what can we learn from your experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: From my experience that-- I learned that you should never give up. You should always hope, no matter how dark the times. SHLOMO (GRANDSON): Umi, I move there. But things will get better. SHLOMO (GRANDSON): I want-- I want to move. And I guess that's the only message. Live your life to the fullest. Enjoy your family, your children, your grandchildren, and things will work out all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for sharing--

speaker: interviewee
text: No one's-- no one's gonna to see this. This is for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. we're going to have an epilogue to our tape. This is Chaya, Gloria's oldest granddaughter. Chaya, what kind of a bubbie is your bubbie? CHAYA (GRANDDAUGHTER): The greatest anyone could ever have in the world. Why is she so wonderful? What does she do that's so great? CHAYA (GRANDDAUGHTER): Everything. Everything she does makes us feel good. And there's not much you could say except that she's great in every single way. You love living so close to her? CHAYA (GRANDDAUGHTER): Mm-hmm. Yeah, and it's also like-- it's almost like an amusement park, or it's as fun to come here and play. Any day that we're off and they're home, we'd rather come here than many other places. Chaya, do you know about your grandmother's life when she was about your age? CHAYA (GRANDDAUGHTER): A little, not much. Mostly-- Can you imagine that you were-- if-- if you were a little girl like-- and had the experiences like she had, can you imagine yourself in that position? CHAYA (GRANDDAUGHTER): I don't think I can. And I also think that, like, no one could have as much courage as someone like that. I don't know if anyone-- if we could have-- if I would be able to. You have a very special lady. Are you proud to be her granddaughter? CHAYA (GRANDDAUGHTER): Yeah. Thank you very much for adding this to your tape. CHAYA (GRANDDAUGHTER): You're welcome.
